segments.caption: The video opens with a glitchy, abstract transition featuring fragmented visuals and the WSJ logo, quickly shifting to a close-up of a hand tapping a payment terminal displaying a $1.00 transaction. The scene transitions to a laptop screen showing a detailed stadium seating map, overlaid with a live concert crowd, suggesting real-time data visualization. Next, a hand holds a smartphone displaying Google Maps with a route from San Francisco to Union Square, set against a city street with trolley tracks and a truck. The video then cuts to a dynamic, animated starfield with song titles like 'Anti-Hero' and 'Karma' labeled, indicating a music visualization. A pair of Bose headphones appears with an 'Outer Microphone' label, followed by a close-up of a human eye with colorful dots, possibly representing biometric data. A man in a dark shirt is seen working at a table with tools, then the scene shifts to a split-screen: a person holding a phone with a route map on the left, and a vibrant purple and pink abstract background on the right. The video continues with a woman in a red top using Google Maps on her phone, then a close-up of a hand typing 'Beverly Hills' into a search bar. A collage of transportation images—traffic lights, trains, highways—appears, followed by a woman walking while using Google Maps on her phone. The final frames show a computer screen with Google Maps directions from '123 main street, chicago' to 'chicago airport,' with options for 'By car' and 'By public transit,' and a woman in a purple jacket using augmented reality navigation on her phone, with a blue arrow guiding her path on the screen.

segments.caption: The video opens with a man in a gray polo shirt speaking directly to the camera in a lab-like setting, surrounded by robotic devices, including a blue and black robot labeled 'Google Maps' and another with a green top. The scene transitions to a close-up of a smartphone displaying Google Maps with augmented reality navigation, showing a real-world street view with a blue arrow and street name overlay. This is followed by an animated sequence of a woman walking down a street while using her phone for navigation, then a close-up of a hand interacting with a smartphone screen showing a map with location pins. The video then cuts to a workshop where a man in a blue shirt adjusts a robotic camera device, followed by a shot of a mounted camera system in a red-walled room. A title card appears: 'THE TECH BEHIND GOOGLE MAPS,' over a colorful abstract background. The next sequence shows a retro-style Google Maps interface from the early 2000s, with a hand typing 'La Jolla, San Diego, CA' into the search bar, then selecting 'Get Directions' and entering '123 main' as a starting point. The interface switches between 'Map,' 'Satellite,' and 'Hybrid' views, and a hand clicks on 'Street View' to reveal a 360-degree street-level image. The video concludes with a man in a vest and glasses speaking in an office with world maps on the wall, then working at a desk with a computer, and finally a shot of globes on a shelf, emphasizing the global scope of mapping technology.

segments.caption: The video opens with a close-up of a hand using a black computer mouse on a vintage-style map mousepad, suggesting a blend of old-world cartography and modern technology. The scene transitions to Craig Dalton, an Associate Professor at Hofstra University, speaking in an office adorned with large world maps, including a detailed political map of Africa, and rolled maps stored vertically on a rack. He appears engaged and animated as he discusses mapping technologies. The narrative then shifts to historical context, showing a hand placing a red pushpin on a physical map of California, followed by a screenshot of a Google blog post from May 28, 2009, detailing the acquisition of Where 2 Tech, a startup that contributed to Google Maps. This is followed by a Wall Street Journal headline from October 27, 2004, announcing Google’s acquisition of Keyhole, a pivotal moment in the evolution of digital mapping. The video then showcases the progression of Google Maps through various interfaces: a 2007 web version with Street View, a smartphone displaying navigation to 'Frankies Spuntino,' and a car’s dashboard screen showing route options to Shenandoah National Park. The final scenes transition to a workshop environment with red walls and exposed beams, where a man in a gray polo shirt gestures toward robotic camera devices, including one with a blue and black body labeled 'Google Maps,' under a 'Fleet Assembly' banner. The setting suggests a behind-the-scenes look at the development of mapping technology, with robotic systems likely used for data collection.

segments.caption: The video opens with a vibrant, digitally stylized title card reading 'THE TECH BEHIND SPOTIFY' against a purple and pink background with binary code, establishing a tech-focused theme. It transitions to a montage of early music streaming interfaces, including a blue-tinted iTunes screen displaying Ray Charles songs, followed by a close-up of a smartphone showing the Pandora app with a 'Start listening for free' prompt. The sequence continues with a hand holding a smartphone displaying the Last.fm app, then shifts to a laptop screen showing the Spotify interface with a green background and various music recommendations. The scene cuts to Thomas Hodgson, Assistant Professor of Musicology at UCLA, speaking in a warmly lit room with wooden acoustic panels, identified by on-screen text. He is later shown reading a book titled 'COMPUTING TASTE' while seated at a desk by a window. The video then shows a hand scrolling through Spotify’s 'Discover Weekly' playlist on a smartphone, followed by a TikTok clip of a man in a striped shirt and cap reacting enthusiastically to the playlist with the text 'you’ve done it again.' The final shot reveals a Spotify office environment with a large green Spotify logo on the wall, a man working at a desk, and a screen displaying 'the echonest' logo, indicating the company’s music recommendation technology.

The segment concludes with the model highlighting 'All I Want for Christmas Is You' by Mariah Carey, underscoring the algorithm’s ability to map diverse musical tastes. segments.caption: The video opens with John Rule, Consulting Engineer at Bose, seated in a bright, modern office with large windows overlooking greenery, wearing a gray suit and white shirt. He speaks directly to the camera, gesturing as he explains noise cancellation technology, with a pair of vintage-style headphones displayed on a stand beside him. The scene transitions to archival footage from the Smithsonian Institution, showing a pilot and a young child in an aircraft cockpit, both wearing bulky aviation headphones, emphasizing the historical roots of noise reduction. The video then cuts to a close-up of John Rule from behind as he puts on a pair of modern Bose headphones, followed by a sleek animated sequence on a black background illustrating the inner workings of active noise cancellation. The sequence concludes with John Rule back in the office, continuing his explanation, then holding up a small pair of black earbuds with blue accents, demonstrating the technology’s evolution into compact form. The WSJ logo remains consistently in the top right corner throughout.

segments.caption: The video opens with a first-person perspective from inside a vehicle, looking out at a savanna landscape with giraffes and trees under a vibrant blue sky, suggesting a virtual or immersive experience. Deanan Dasilva, Principal at Big Sky Technology, Sphere Entertainment, is then seen adjusting the camera, demonstrating its functionality. The segment concludes with him handling a single camera module, underscoring the technical craftsmanship involved in creating these immersive experiences.

The video transitions to abstract data visualizations—first a spectrogram with orange dots, then a grid of blue dots with connecting lines—before revealing the title card 'THE TECH BEHIND SHAZAM' over a vibrant digital background. segments.caption: The video opens with Chris Barton, Co-founder of Shazam, speaking directly to the camera in a modern office setting with a cityscape visible through large windows behind him. He is wearing a light blue button-up shirt and gestures as he explains the technology. The scene transitions to a close-up of a smartphone displaying the Shazam app interface with the word 'Listening' and the Shazam logo, overlaid with a large white Apple logo, indicating Apple’s acquisition of Shazam. A title card from The Wall Street Journal appears, reading 'Apple Acquires Shazam and Its Song-Recognition App,' dated December 11, 2017, with the byline 'Tripp Mickle.' The video returns to Chris Barton, who continues speaking and gesturing. He then demonstrates the Shazam app on his phone, holding it up to listen for music. The screen shows the app’s interface, including a 'Listening for music' prompt, followed by a display of recognized songs like 'Después de la Playa' by Bad Bunny and 'Wild Child' by The Black Keys, with Apple Music buttons. The app’s history screen is shown, listing songs such as 'Riptide' by Vance Joy and 'Free' by The Machine. The video then displays the Shazam icon within Apple’s Control Center, emphasizing integration. Chris Barton smiles and nods as he concludes his explanation. The video ends with black screens listing credits for footage and animation, including names like Karl Mollohan, Corey Eisenstein, Sean Harvey, Ryan Trefes, Michael Tabb, Luca Depardon, and others, with sources such as Google, Getty Images, and Shazam.
